evidence.quote: For rulers are not a terror to good works, but to the evil. Wilt thou then not be afraid of the power? do that which is good, and thou shalt have praise of the same:
evidence.reasoning: Defines the proper role of rulers—to reward good and punish evil; when they invert this (terrorizing the good), they step outside their God-given office.

evidence.quote: For he is the minister of God to thee for good. But if thou do that which is evil, be afraid; for he beareth not the sword in vain: for he is the minister of God, a revenger to execute wrath upon him that doeth evil.
evidence.reasoning: Twice calls the ruler 'minister of God'—for good and for wrath on evil; implies authority is bounded by these purposes and loses divine sanction when perverted.

evidence.quote: Owe no man any thing, but to love one another: for he that loveth another hath fulfilled the law.
evidence.reasoning: Immediately following civil-obedience teaching, Paul grounds all obligation in love and the moral law, suggesting that civil duties cannot contradict the law of love.

evidence.quote: For this, Thou shalt not commit adultery, Thou shalt not kill, Thou shalt not steal… it is briefly comprehended in this saying, namely, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself.
evidence.reasoning: Lists the moral law that fulfills love; if a government commands killing the innocent or stealing, it contradicts the neighbor-love that Paul just invoked as the sum of Christian duty.

evidence.quote: Shall even he that hateth right govern? and wilt thou condemn him that is most just?
evidence.reasoning: Rhetorical question implying that one who hates justice has no right to govern, supporting the idea that governance is morally conditioned.

positions.label: Prudential submission with passive resistance: obey when possible, disobey quietly when conscience requires
positions.summary: Romans 13 counsels general submission to preserve order and witness, but does not require believers to obey commands that violate God's law. When authorities demand sin, Christians may refuse and accept the penalty ('for conscience sake,' v. 5), but should avoid active rebellion or insurrection. The focus is on enduring unjust suffering rather than overthrowing unjust regimes.
positions.why_not_higher: The text does not distinguish between active rebellion and passive disobedience; verse 2's warning that 'they that resist shall receive to themselves damnation' may apply to any resistance, not merely violent revolt, and Paul gives no worked example of conscientious refusal in the passage.
positions.confidence_rationale: Verse 5 ('for conscience sake') opens the door to conscience-driven limits, but the passage does not explain whether 'conscience' can override the command to 'be subject' or only explains why one submits; much depends on how one reads the grammar and Paul's assumed context.

evidence.quote: Wherefore ye must needs be subject, not only for wrath, but also for conscience sake.
evidence.reasoning: The mention of conscience as a ground for submission implies that Christian conscience is engaged, which in turn implies it may prohibit obedience when commanded to sin.

evidence.quote: Let every soul be subject unto the higher powers. For there is no power but of God: the powers that be are ordained of God.
evidence.reasoning: God's ordination of authorities means even unjust rulers serve His sovereign purposes; believers submit as far as possible while trusting God's providence.

evidence.quote: The Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations, and to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished:
evidence.reasoning: God will judge the unjust in His time; believers need not take vengeance or rebellion into their own hands.

positions.label: Contextual reading: Paul addresses routine civic duties under Roman rule, not tyranny or persecution
positions.summary: Romans 13:1–7 is a practical instruction for believers living under stable (if pagan) Roman administration, focusing on taxes, customs, and ordinary law. Paul assumes a relatively just order where doing good brings praise (v. 3). The passage does not speak to scenarios of active persecution, genocide, or commands to apostatize, and should not be universalized beyond its situational scope.
positions.why_not_higher: Verse 1 uses universal language ('every soul,' 'the powers that be are ordained of God') with no textual signal that it applies only to benign regimes; the burden is on the interpreter to import context from outside the passage, which is a weaker move than reading the passage on its own terms.
positions.confidence_rationale: The passage does not self-limit its scope, and historical context must be imported from external knowledge; the text itself is written as principle, not case law.

evidence.quote: For for this cause pay ye tribute also: for they are God's ministers, attending continually upon this very thing.
evidence.reasoning: Concrete focus on tax-paying suggests Paul is addressing mundane civic compliance, not heroic resistance to totalitarian evil.

evidence.quote: Render therefore to all their dues: tribute to whom tribute is due; custom to whom custom; fear to whom fear; honour to whom honour.
evidence.reasoning: Enumerates ordinary civic obligations (taxes, customs, respect), implying a normal-functioning government rather than an oppressive regime.

evidence.quote: For rulers are not a terror to good works, but to the evil.
evidence.reasoning: Describes the Roman magistrates Paul's readers actually faced—generally indifferent to Christian piety, punishing crime—not Nero's later persecution or modern totalitarianism.

result.dissent_notes: The central interpretive fork is whether Romans 13:3–4 (rulers as 'ministers of God for good,' 'not a terror to good works') is (a) a normative definition that limits the scope of vv. 1–2, or (b) a descriptivist generalization that assumes most governments function justly most of the time. If (a), then unjust commands fall outside 'the powers that be' and v. 2's prohibition of resistance does not apply; if (b), then v. 2 binds even under tyranny, and believers submit while God judges. Paul does not adjudicate this. The 'qualified submission' position (50%) receives the highest weight because vv. 3–4 are integrated into the argument ('For rulers are…'), not a parenthetical, and because the immediate context (vv. 8–10) invokes the moral law as the boundary of all Christian obligation. Yet the 'absolute submission' reading (18%) has a plain-sense claim on vv. 1–2, and the 'prudential/passive' reading (26%) offers a via media. The 'contextual' reading (6%) is plausible but relies heavily on external data and sits uneasily with the universal grammar of v. 1. Confidence is medium, not high, because Paul nowhere illustrates what to do when a ruler commands evil, and the passage was written in a setting where that question was not yet acute for Roman Christians.
unresolved_tensions: Whether 'the powers that be are ordained of God' (v. 1) means every regime exercises legitimate authority, or only that God sovereignly permits even wicked rulers without endorsing their acts.
Whether 'they that resist shall receive to themselves damnation' (v. 2) applies to active rebellion only, or to any disobedience including conscientious refusal of unjust commands.
Whether vv. 3–4 (rulers reward good and punish evil) describe the purpose that legitimates authority or merely Paul's empirical observation of first-century Roman governance.
How to integrate the moral law of neighbor-love (vv. 8–10) with the command to submit (vv. 1–5) when the two conflict—does love limit submission, or does submission express love by preserving order?
result.synthesis: The strongest absolute-submission argument (18%) reads vv. 1–2 as categorical divine command with no exception clauses: God ordains all authority, resistance is sin, and believers submit even to unjust rulers while trusting God's judgment (2 Peter 2:9). The strongest qualified-submission argument (50%) treats vv. 3–4 as definitional: rulers are 'ministers of God for good' and lose divine sanction when they become a terror to good works, so obedience is owed only within that mandate, bounded by the moral law of neighbor-love (vv. 8–10). The central tension is grammatical and theological: does 'for' in v. 3 introduce the reason all powers must be obeyed, or the condition under which they function as God's ministers? The passage does not resolve whether a ruler who commands evil is still 'the power' of v. 2, nor whether 'conscience sake' (v. 5) can override 'be subject.' Both traditions read the same text; they parse its logic differently.
result.confidence_rationale: Romans 13:1–7 directly addresses the question and provides multiple relevant verses with clear imperatives, but the passage does not supply a worked example of unjust government or explicit instruction for conflicting commands. The interpretive weight falls on how one integrates vv. 3–4 (descriptive or prescriptive?) with vv. 1–2 (absolute or conditional?), and Paul does not make that move explicit. The retrieval includes much low-relevance noise (Proverbs on 'apply,' Acts on Roman citizenship) and no cross-referencing texts that show apostles disobeying unjust orders (e.g., Acts 4–5), which would raise confidence. The question is well-defined, the primary text is present, but key interpretive moves depend on theological tradition rather than additional explicit scripture.

results.question: What does Scripture teach about assurance of salvation?

evidence.text: From infancy, you have known the holy Scriptures which are able to make you wise for salvation through faith which is in Christ Jesus.
And hereby we know that we are of the truth, and shall assure our hearts before him. {assure: Gr. persuade}
The LORD’s angel solemnly assured Joshua, saying,
Now faith is assurance ofthingshoped for, a conviction of things not seen.

Then said I, Ah, Lord GOD! behold, the prophets say unto them, Ye shall not see the sword, neither shall ye have famine; but I will give you assured peace in this place. {assured...: Heb. peace of truth}
and being fully assured that what he had promised, he was able also to perform.
YetGod preserveth the mighty by his power: He riseth up that hath no assurance of life.
And the work of righteousness shall be peace; and the effect of righteousness quietness and assurance for ever.
Then opened he their understanding, that they might understand the scriptures,
which he promised afore through his prophets in the holy scriptures,
The Scripture was fulfilled which says, “He was counted with transgressors.”
Assur also is joined with them: they have holpen the children of Lot. Selah. {holpen: Heb. been an arm to}
For the scripture saith, Whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed.
How then should the scriptures be fulfilled, that thus it must be?
knowing this first, that no prophecy of scripture is of private interpretation.
Again another Scripture says, “They will look on him whom they pierced.”
But continue thou in the things which thou hast learned and hast been assured of, knowing of whom thou hast learned {them};
And we desire that every one of you do shew the same diligence to the full assurance of hope unto the end:
One man esteemeth one day above another: another esteemeth every day alike. Let each man be fully assured in his own mind.
But how then shall the scriptures be fulfilled, that thus it must be?

positions.label: Assurance is grounded in faith and scriptural promise
positions.summary: Believers can have assurance of salvation by trusting in God's promises revealed in Scripture. Faith itself provides conviction about unseen realities, and Scripture testifies that those who believe will not be put to shame. Assurance flows from believing what God has promised and recognizing His ability to fulfill it.
positions.why_not_higher: The provided evidence does not include direct NT passages that explicitly state 'you can know with certainty you are saved' or address pastoral concerns about false assurance, leaving some interpretive work to connect faith-promises to subjective assurance.
positions.confidence_rationale: Multiple verses directly link faith to salvific knowledge and divine promises, though the step from 'Scripture makes wise unto salvation through faith' to 'I personally possess assured salvation' requires modest inference.

evidence.quote: the holy Scriptures which are able to make you wise for salvation through faith which is in Christ Jesus
evidence.reasoning: Scripture itself is the instrument that imparts salvific wisdom through faith, grounding assurance in an objective, external authority.

evidence.quote: Whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed
evidence.reasoning: Scripture promises that the one who believes will not be disappointed, providing a foundation for confidence in one's standing before God.

evidence.quote: being fully assured that what he had promised, he was able also to perform
evidence.reasoning: Abraham's example shows that full assurance rests on God's power to keep His promises, modeling how believers can be certain of salvation based on divine faithfulness.

evidence.quote: faith is assurance of things hoped for, a conviction of things not seen
evidence.reasoning: Faith inherently involves assurance and conviction about invisible realities (including salvation), suggesting assurance is integral to genuine faith rather than optional.

positions.label: Assurance is confirmed through self-examination and godly living
positions.summary: Believers gain assurance by examining whether they possess the marks of genuine faith—living according to truth and showing diligence in hope. Assurance is not merely intellectual consent to promises but involves behavioral and heart-level confirmation that one's faith is real.
positions.why_not_higher: The evidence set is thin on passages that explicitly develop the role of works/fruit in assurance (e.g., missing James 2, 1 John 2–3 on obedience as a test). The one directly relevant verse (1 John 3:19) is present but not surrounded by broader epistolary context.
positions.confidence_rationale: 1 John 3:19 explicitly ties assurance to living 'of the truth,' and Hebrews 6:11 links diligence to full assurance of hope, but the retrieval lacks comprehensive passages on practical tests of faith.

evidence.quote: And hereby we know that we are of the truth, and shall assure our hearts before him
evidence.reasoning: Assurance ('hereby we know') is connected to being 'of the truth'—implying that moral and relational alignment with truth provides the basis for a settled heart before God.

evidence.quote: we desire that every one of you do shew the same diligence to the full assurance of hope unto the end
evidence.reasoning: Full assurance of hope is linked to diligence, suggesting that persevering effort in the Christian life is part of how believers attain and maintain assurance.

evidence.quote: continue thou in the things which thou hast learned and hast been assured of
evidence.reasoning: Timothy's assurance is tied to continuing in what he learned, implying that steadfastness in teaching and practice sustains confidence in salvation.

positions.label: Assurance is the fruit of righteousness and peace with God
positions.summary: Assurance is not a subjective feeling or doctrinal claim but the objective fruit of righteousness—peace, quietness, and lasting confidence. It flows from a life that reflects God's work of righteousness and is experienced as settled rest rather than anxious striving.
positions.why_not_higher: The only directly relevant verse (Isaiah 32:17) is from the OT prophets and speaks in corporate/eschatological terms about the effect of righteousness, requiring substantial hermeneutical bridging to apply to individual NT soteriology.
positions.confidence_rationale: Isaiah 32:17 clearly connects righteousness to lasting assurance, but as a single prophetic verse it offers limited basis for a full position without corroborating NT development.

evidence.quote: the work of righteousness shall be peace; and the effect of righteousness quietness and assurance for ever
evidence.reasoning: Assurance is presented as the enduring effect of righteousness—suggesting it is not self-generated emotion but the natural outcome of God's transforming work in a person's life.

positions.label: Assurance is uncertain or not universally promised in this life
positions.summary: Some texts suggest that even the mighty or those who appear strong may lack assurance of life, and human confidence can be misplaced. This minority view holds that absolute certainty may not be attainable or appropriate given human frailty and the hidden nature of election.
positions.why_not_higher: Job 24:22 is a single poetic observation about God's sovereignty over the mighty, not a doctrinal assertion about Christian assurance. It is outweighed by multiple NT texts affirming confident faith and divine promise.
positions.confidence_rationale: The verse is real but contextually about God's inscrutable governance, not a positive teaching that believers should lack assurance; it functions better as a caution than a position.

evidence.quote: Yet God preserveth the mighty by his power: He riseth up that hath no assurance of life
evidence.reasoning: Even the powerful can lack assurance of life, illustrating human fragility and suggesting that certainty about one's standing is not always given, even to those who seem secure.

synthesis.dissent_notes: The retrieval lacks many classic assurance texts (Rom 8:16, 38–39; 1 John 5:13; John 10:28–29; Eph 1:13–14) and texts on perseverance (Phil 1:6) or warning (Heb 6:4–6), making it difficult to weigh eternal-security vs. conditional-perseverance traditions fairly. The minority 'uncertain assurance' position is represented by a single Job verse of ambiguous relevance. Several verses are retrieval noise (e.g., Ps 83:8 on 'Assur' = Assyria; verses mentioning 'scripture' generically). Confidence is medium-to-low because the question is theologically contested and the evidence is incomplete.
unresolved_tensions: Whether assurance is immediate upon faith (arising from the promise alone) or develops through spiritual growth and self-examination over time.
How to reconcile the call to 'full assurance' with biblical warnings against presumption and the reality that some professing believers fall away.

The relationship between objective grounds for assurance (God's promises, Christ's work) and subjective grounds (the Spirit's witness, experiential fruit)—evidence set does not include passages on the Spirit's internal witness (Rom 8:16, Gal 4:6).
synthesis.synthesis: The strongest position (50%) sees assurance rooted in faith and the scriptural promise that believers will not be ashamed (Rom 10:11), modeled by Abraham's full persuasion in God's ability to perform what He promised (Rom 4:21). This view treats assurance as intrinsic to faith ('faith is assurance,' Heb 11:1) and grounded in the external, objective authority of Scripture (2 Tim 3:15). A significant alternative (28%) emphasizes that assurance is confirmed and sustained through diligent Christian living and self-examination ('hereby we know that we are of the truth,' 1 John 3:19; 'full assurance of hope' linked to diligence, Heb 6:11). The central interpretive tension is whether assurance is primarily a matter of believing the promise (external, immediate) or examining the fruit (internal, progressive). A smaller voice (16%) sees assurance as the experiential fruit of righteousness—peace and quietness (Isa 32:17)—rather than a cognitive claim. A minority caution (6%) from Job 24:22 warns that even the mighty may lack assurance of life, though this is a weak basis for a full position. The evidence set is insufficient to adjudicate between major Reformed, Arminian, and Catholic traditions on assurance, and the absence of key NT texts (Rom 8, 1 John 5:13, perseverance passages) limits confidence.
synthesis.confidence_rationale: Multiple relevant verses support the leading positions, but the retrieval is incomplete (missing Rom 8:16, 1 John 5:13, warning passages) and includes noise. The question is theologically controverted with distinct traditions interpreting the same texts differently (Reformed 'full assurance' vs. Catholic 'moral certainty' vs. Arminian 'conditional security'). The evidence allows defensible positions but does not resolve the central debate about the nature and basis of assurance.

results.question: How should the Sermon on the Mount relate to Christian ethics?

evidence.text: He said to the people, “Be ready by the third day. Don’t have sexual relations with a woman.”
They related the things that happened along the way, and how he was recognized by them in the breaking of the bread.
And Moses went up into the mount, and a cloud covered the mount.
And Jehovah came down upon mount Sinai, to the top of the mount: and Jehovah called Moses to the top of the mount; and Moses went up.
And Abijah stood up upon mount Zemaraim, which {is} in mount Ephraim, and said, Hear me, thou Jeroboam, and all Israel;
And saviours shall come up on mount Zion to judge the mount of Esau; and the kingdom shall be Jehovah’s.

{Even} from the mount Halak, that goeth up to Seir, even unto Baalgad in the valley of Lebanon under mount Hermon: and all their kings he took, and smote them, and slew them. {the mount...: or, the smooth mountain}

Then Laban overtook Jacob. Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the mount: and Laban with his brethren pitched in the mount of Gilead.
When they stood, these stood. When they mounted up, these mounted up with them; for the spirit of the living creature was in them.
The LORD said to Moses on Mount Sinai,
And Moses entered into the midst of the cloud, and went up into the mount: and Moses was in the mount forty days and forty nights.
And Moses said unto the LORD, The people cannot come up to mount Sinai: for thou chargedst us, saying, Set bounds about the mount, and sanctify it.
And be ready in the morning, and come up in the morning unto mount Sinai, and present thyself there to me in the top of the mount.
So I turned and came down from the mount, and the mount burned with fire: and the two tables of the covenant {were} in my two hands.
And they appointed Kedesh in Galilee in mount Naphtali, and Shechem in mount Ephraim, and Kirjatharba, which {is} Hebron, in the mountain of Judah. {appointed: Heb. sanctified}
Thus dwelt Esau in mount Seir: Esau {is} Edom.
They traveled from Kehelathah, and encamped in Mount Shepher.
They traveled from Mount Shepher, and encamped in Haradah.
They traveled from Mount Hor, and encamped in Zalmonah.
And no man shall come up with thee, neither let any man be seen throughout all the mount; neither let the flocks nor herds feed before that mount.
And it came to pass, when he was come nigh to Bethphage and Bethany, at the mount called {the mount} of Olives, he sent two of his disciples,
the five lords of the Philistines, all the Canaanites, the Sidonians, and the Hivites who lived on Mount Lebanon, from Mount Baal Hermon to the entrance of Hamath.

positions.label: Literal-Direct Ethic (Anabaptist / Radical Discipleship)
positions.summary: The Sermon on the Mount is Jesus's binding ethical code for all disciples in all times, to be obeyed literally and directly without qualification or contextualization.
positions.why_not_higher: No direct textual evidence from the Sermon itself appears in the candidate set; this position relies entirely on Matthew 5–7 passages absent from retrieval, making it impossible to cite supporting verses and limiting weight despite its historical prominence.
positions.confidence_rationale: Cannot cite any verse from the candidate evidence because the retrieval returned only geographic/narrative 'mount' references, not the ethical content of Matthew 5–7.

positions.label: Impossible Ideal / Convicting Mirror (Lutheran Two-Kingdom)
positions.summary: The Sermon functions primarily to expose human sinfulness and drive believers to grace, not as a practicable ethic; it shows what God demands but cannot be fulfilled apart from Christ's righteousness.
positions.why_not_higher: Like the first position, this view depends on reading Matthew 5–7 (e.g., 'Be perfect,' the antitheses), none of which appear in the candidate evidence; weight remains low because no verse_id can be cited.
positions.confidence_rationale: The candidate evidence contains no passages from the Sermon on the Mount or Pauline texts on justification that would undergird this interpretive tradition.

positions.label: Interim Ethic (Schweitzer / Eschatological Postponement)
positions.summary: Jesus gave these radical commands expecting the imminent arrival of God's kingdom; they are suited to a brief interim period, not normative for ongoing Christian life in history.
positions.why_not_higher: No eschatological or kingdom-imminence texts from Matthew or the Gospels appear in the evidence; the position cannot be anchored in any cited verse_id.
positions.confidence_rationale: Schweitzer's thesis requires Matthew 5–7 plus kingdom-timing sayings (e.g., Matt 10:23, 16:28), none of which are present.

positions.label: Disposition / Attitude Ethic (Augustinian Internalization)
positions.summary: The Sermon prescribes inner attitudes (humility, mercy, purity of heart) rather than external legal compliance; love fulfills the deeper spirit of the commands even when outward form differs.
positions.why_not_higher: No Sermon text (Matt 5:8, 5:21–22, 5:27–28) or love-summaries (Matt 22:37–40) appear in the evidence to demonstrate the attitude/spirit distinction.
positions.confidence_rationale: This reading depends on verses contrasting external act and internal motive, which the candidate set does not include.

positions.label: General Principles Requiring Prudential Application
positions.summary: The Sermon offers broad moral principles (non-retaliation, generosity, truthfulness) that must be contextualized through wisdom and circumstances rather than applied as universal rules.
positions.why_not_higher: Without access to the antitheses (Matt 5:38–42) or the 'do not resist evil' command, we cannot cite the specific texts that defenders invoke to argue for principled flexibility.
positions.confidence_rationale: The evidence set lacks both the Sermon passages and New Testament prudential texts (e.g., Paul's case-by-case counsel in 1 Corinthians) needed to support this position.

positions.label: Monastic / Counsels-of-Perfection (Catholic Dual-Track)
positions.summary: The Sermon's most rigorous demands (poverty, celibacy, non-resistance) are 'counsels of perfection' binding on those with a special vocation (religious orders), not universal precepts for all Christians.
positions.why_not_higher: Medieval exegesis of Matt 5:48 and 19:21 is required to ground the precept/counsel distinction; none of those verses appear in the candidate evidence.
positions.confidence_rationale: This position relies on texts distinguishing commandments from supererogatory counsels, which are absent from the retrieval.

positions.label: Kingdom Ethic Already-and-Not-Yet (Inaugurated Eschatology)
positions.summary: The Sermon describes the ethics of God's kingdom, which has broken into the present age in Jesus but awaits consummation; Christians live the kingdom life now as a sign and foretaste, empowered by the Spirit.
positions.why_not_higher: This synthetic view requires both Sermon content (Matt 5:3–10, 6:10, 6:33) and inaugurated-kingdom texts (Luke 11:20, Rom 14:17); the evidence contains none.
positions.confidence_rationale: Without Matthew 5–7 or Paul's 'already/not yet' language, the position cannot be textually anchored in the candidate set.

synthesis.dissent_notes: The candidate evidence consists almost entirely of geographic references to 'mount' (Sinai, Zion, Olives, etc.) and contains zero verses from Matthew 5–7 (the Sermon on the Mount itself) or from New Testament ethical discourse. Consequently, every major interpretive tradition—Anabaptist literalism, Lutheran law/gospel dialectic, Schweitzer's interim ethic, Augustinian dispositional reading, prudential-application models, Catholic dual-track, and inaugurated-eschatology syntheses—remains entirely unsupported by citeable text. The weights above reflect the relative strength and historical influence of each tradition within Christian theology, not the evidence retrieved, because no evidence was retrieved. A rigorous answer to 'How should the Sermon relate to Christian ethics?' requires access to the Sermon; this retrieval returned none.
unresolved_tensions: Literal obedience vs. grace-centered impossibility: Does the Sermon set forth achievable commands or a mirror to expose sin and point to Christ?
Universal norm vs. vocational counsel: Are the radical demands (e.g., Matt 5:39–42) binding on every believer or reserved for those with a special calling?
Timeless principle vs. eschatological context: Did Jesus intend these teachings for an imminent kingdom or for the ongoing life of the church across history?
Outward compliance vs. inner disposition: Does obedience mean performing the specified acts or cultivating the heart-attitudes they express?
Individual piety vs. social-political witness: Is the Sermon addressed to personal sanctification, to the community's corporate life, or to engagement with the surrounding world?
Already-inaugurated vs. not-yet-consummated kingdom: How does the overlap of ages affect the Sermon's application—do we live it now or await its full realization?
synthesis.synthesis: Seven major traditions offer competing frameworks for relating the Sermon on the Mount to Christian ethics. **Literal-Direct** advocates (Anabaptists, Mennonites) read Matthew 5–7 as Jesus's plain instructions for disciples—'turn the other cheek' means exactly that. **Impossible-Ideal** interpreters (Luther, classic Protestantism) argue the Sermon's perfection (Matt 5:48) convicts us of sin and drives us to justification by faith, not a to-do list. **Interim-Ethic** scholars (Schweitzer) situate the commands in Jesus's expectation of the imminent end, rendering them time-bound. **Disposition-Ethic** readers (Augustine, much of the tradition) locate the Sermon's force in attitudes—humility, purity of motive—rather than external rules. **Prudential-Application** models treat the Sermon as general principles requiring contextual wisdom. **Counsels-of-Perfection** (medieval Catholicism) reserve the most demanding sayings for those under vows. **Inaugurated-Kingdom** frameworks (Ladd, Wright) see the Sermon as the ethic of God's reign breaking into the present, to be lived now in the power of the Spirit and awaiting full realization. The central interpretive tension is whether the Sermon is **direct law** (to be obeyed as stated) or **theological pointer** (law that convicts, ideal that inspires, or kingdom vision that transcends the present age), and whether its audience is **all Christians** or a **subset with special vocation**. No single reading resolves all these tensions; each captures aspects of the text's rhetoric and function.
synthesis.confidence_rationale: The candidate evidence contains zero verses from the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5–7) and no New Testament ethical or theological commentary that would support any of the classical positions. All retrieved verses are geographic or narrative references to various mountains. The question 'How should the Sermon on the Mount relate to Christian ethics?' is well-defined and has generated centuries of serious exegesis, but the evidence set is completely orthogonal to it. Weights assigned above reflect the relative scholarly and traditional strength of each interpretive school when the full canon is in view, not the non-existent support in this retrieval. Confidence is necessarily low because no position can cite a single relevant verse_id.

evidence_classification.reasoning: Exodus 19:15 (preparation for Sinai theophany) is unrelated to the Sermon on the Mount or Christian ethics.
Luke 24:35 (Emmaus road) concerns resurrection recognition, not ethical teaching or the Sermon.
Exodus 24:15 (Moses ascending Sinai) is geographic/narrative, not relevant to New Testament ethics.
Exodus 19:20 (YHWH descending on Sinai) is part of the Sinai covenant narrative, not the Sermon on the Mount.
2 Chronicles 13:4 (Abijah's speech on Mount Zemaraim) is historical narrative, unrelated to Jesus's ethical teaching.
Obadiah 1:21 (eschatological judgment on Edom) does not address the Sermon or Christian conduct.
Joshua 11:17 (conquest geography) is irrelevant to the Sermon on the Mount or ethics.
John 8:1 (Jesus at the Mount of Olives) is a location note; the pericope that follows (if present) is not the Sermon on the Mount.
Genesis 31:25 (Jacob and Laban on Mount Gilead) is patriarchal narrative, unrelated to Christian ethics.
Ezekiel 10:17 (cherubim's movement) uses 'mounted' in a vision context, not relevant to the Sermon.
Leviticus 25:1 (Sabbath/Jubilee law given on Sinai) is Old Testament legislation, not New Testament ethical instruction.
Exodus 24:18 (Moses on Sinai forty days) is narrative background to the covenant, not the Sermon on the Mount.
Exodus 19:23 (boundary around Sinai) is part of theophany protocol, unrelated to Jesus's teaching.
Exodus 34:2 (Moses ascending Sinai for second tablets) is covenant-renewal narrative, not Christian ethics.
Deuteronomy 9:15 (Moses descending with tablets) is part of Israel's history, not the Sermon on the Mount.
Joshua 20:7 (cities of refuge) is Old Testament legal/geographic detail, unrelated to the Sermon.
Genesis 36:8 (Esau dwelling in Mount Seir) is genealogical/geographic, not ethical or theological.
Numbers 33:23 (wilderness itinerary) is geographic log, unrelated to Christian ethics.
Numbers 33:24 (wilderness itinerary) is geographic log, unrelated to the Sermon on the Mount.
Numbers 33:41 (wilderness itinerary) is geographic log, unrelated to Christian ethics.
Exodus 34:3 (Moses alone on Sinai) is covenant narrative, not relevant to the Sermon or ethics.
Luke 19:29 (Jesus approaching Jerusalem via Mount of Olives) is passion-week narrative, not the Sermon on the Mount.
Judges 3:3 (peoples left in the land, including those on Mount Lebanon) is conquest/settlement history, unrelated to Christian ethics.
